item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The first image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from here with no indication is has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

The second image has been deemed user-generated, and is thus sourced as follows:

Source: SCP Foundation Wiki
License: CC BY-SA 3.0
Title: Molecule.png
Author: SnakeoilSage
Release year: 2013

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a twist on the "memetic language that rewrites your mind" it is interesting, new. The same goes for the twist on "The Foundation's Secret Goal". The idea that the power comes from the Gestalt, the mean will of Humanity instead of a single individuals or a simple majority was a nice balance to what could be otherwise too overpowered to become a good article.

Even then, it got some problems, and one of them is making humanity too powerful.

But not the biggest, mind you. The biggest problem is that it is still a bit confusing. I got by the end that the language actually resets people to a previous state instead of actually being the alien force in their minds, and that the Foundation is actually a branch of this "Alien Foundation". We are Keter-Class objects in a huge Containment Cell, that is clear.

Some key questions remain, though. Why the Foundation agrees with that? Why "affected" people becomes so hostile? Why do their language acts as a counter-memetic agent? There are some good answers to that in the author's post, but their place isn't in the author's post. At least a reasonable and interesting hint to those answers could be in the article, and they aren't.

No vote for now, maybe downvote. A shame, It does have more potential than 2000.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They lose the ability to comprehend or understand any language

Comprehend and understand mean the same thing, don't they?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Minor correction:

a 1-cm ball of ionized plasma, measured at 10,000 kelvin

Plasma is ionized by definition, so "ionized plasma" is confusingly redundant. I'd recommend either changing this to "ionized gas" which is the same as "plasma" by definition, or if that seems like it might not get the point across properly, you can go for "highly ionized plasma" as a middle-ground term or "dense plasma" or "plasma with a high degree of ionization", though I think the latter choices are hard to fit in the existing text. Could also try "ionized gas (plasma)".

+ Nitpicking on temperature
– hide block

The temperature is a bit immersion-breaking for me. 10k kelvin exactly is a bit weird. I understand that obsessively precise numbers are avoided for obvious reasons, but I'd prefer it if this was "approx. 10,000 kelvin" or if the researchers can measure temperature exactly, make it something obviously fitting to a numeral system that is not base-10. If the scale of 10k kelvin isn't fine-tuned in the article, then you can go for 8,192 kelvin or 16,384 kelvin which are obvious to someone familiar with binary. Base-12 is also popular enough as to be recognizable as what, speculatively, a civilization with "superior intellect" (as is implied) might use, however the closest useful power of 12 is 20,736 so that might be too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They are interstellar civilizations in our universe, I thought. I am a little confused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm such a sucker for alternate reality equivalents of the foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, he didn't. His comments only apply if you believe you never had the power. If you believe you will not twenty minutes from now because you damn well say you will not, then it will work just fine. No retroactive changes needed—you had the power, now you don't. Why don't you? Because you said so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The author of the article covered that on page 2 of the comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoting (which makes this hit 100), because it's an interesting concept. That being said, I don't like this being the source of all anomalies, and also, if humans don't have to believe what they cause, it would be very easy for them to say, "Twenty minutes after finishing this sentence, I shall lose my anomalous abilities."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Of course, it would make far more sense to have the true SCP-001 hidden away in the 2000s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whilst I don't accept this as my personal headcanon, it's an excellent idea with a great execution. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do really like the name of this SCP! Good work and well done, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Am I the only one thinking Genius: The Transgression now?

While we're at it, am I the only one that knows what that is here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, did the article title come from Earthworm Jim or elsewhere?
Anyway, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I like this a LOT. I got about 90% of the true canon for this from the article, the one piece I did not pick up on was that humans are latent reality-benders.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I originally tried to reconcile all canons, and there's really only one way to do it. There is no canon… is canon.

You see, I wrote a skip where a guy finds the source code of the Matrix-world that our universe is, messes with it, and glitches himself. Here's what I commented about it:

…it's not that "The Foundation is in a computer program" is my headcanon for Foundation reality, but what is in my headcanon is that the Foundation keeps finding proof concerning the fundamental nature of reality, all for theories fundamentally at odds with each other. Like they'll have indisputable proof that the God of Abraham is the One True Creator, then they'll find proof that we're one of a billion realities, then another piece of evidence that shows we're just a dream of some horrible Lovecraftian, and yet another proof showing SCP-343 controls everything, then another showing that we're all in a computer simulation.

So this skip is proof that we used to be a galactic superpower, while SCP-1000 is proof that we're only recently evolved alongside Bigfoot and SCP-343 is proof that God loves us and wants us to be happy. The Foundation knows all of it, and most of the Foundation, including the O5s, have no idea what to believe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"You're looking at it too much from those old grade school videos about people who wish zinc out of existence or something, and then they wake up and everything's normal yet all these objects that rely on zinc no longer function and no one can remember it existing yet never bother to explain why they still built objects that don't work without a non-existent element."

I wish I could upvote comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to say just that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got it. :) Thanks for pointing it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So…can I fix the "0-5" thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's okay. Maybe we can post instructions on the Contest page so no one else makes the mistake, or maybe it was just me. lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And then eeeeeeeeeeeeverything was aaaaaaaall better. Sorry for the confusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for your help in moving the article, Eskobar!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because when you learn Santa Claus isn't real, your memories and experiences related to him don't disappear. Your life is still shaped by that believe. To remove the myth of Santa Claus entirely from your existence would be to erase him retroactively so that his presence at any time would have had an affect on you.

Therefore, if the power exists and you disbelieve it out of existence, it doesn't just "stop" or shut off at that point in the flow of time because it still existed, can be proven to exist, and thus you might believe it was real again. It has to remove itself from all potentialities including how it affected you and all aspects of reality, fundamentally rewriting history and all the infinite possible timelines in which it existed.

And that's impossible to do on its own, by the way, since if there are infinite possible universes then it stands to reason that there are infinite possible universes where the power still exists and thus affects even those universes where it does, because it is part of the infinitude. For that reason alone it could not delete itself without going back to the very beginning and restarting all realities.

You're looking at it too much from those old grade school videos about people who wish zinc out of existence or something, and then they wake up and everything's normal yet all these objects that rely on zinc no longer function and no one can remember it existing yet never bother to explain why they still built objects that don't work without a non-existent element.

Ergo, for it to cease to exist would be to remove itself from having ever existed, and since it can't do that without first existing, paradox.

And on top of all of this, the point of containing humanity isn't to strip them of their ability. It's to keep them from damaging reality while they mature into it. The same issue is pointed out in the Warhammer 40,000 universe. Psykers basically have the same power I'm describing here, but humanity is so immature that it is inadvertently creating daemons to prey on them. The theme of the Warhammer universes is that we are our own worst enemy because the daemons are the creations of our own grimdark minds.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *Shrug* Some people vote on ideas, some people vote on technicalities. No grief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's never a bad idea to post constructive ideas.

My experience with the site has been "there is no canon." I think it's the only way to stay sane when a cyborg Biblical Cain is living in the same universe as pan-dimensional vending machines that sell barbequed aliens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea enough to upvote, but a lot of the language isn't clinical enough for the Foundation. Some edits would be good. Otherwise, thumbs up~!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm holding my votes until near the end, after I've read all the entries, but I'm leaning toward a downvote. My reason is that I like the idea of coexisting headcanons. I'm still new, but it feels like Foundation Tales are the place to tell something that takes place exclusively within one headcanon. I think I like the headcanon, you're introducing, but this SCP turns the setting on its head and doesn't really leave room for other explanations and other headcanons. The SCP-001 proposals break this, but in-setting, some or all of them are presumably decoys.

Ideally, I'd like to see more ambiguity.

I think if I type one more headcanon, my headcanon will headcanon…

Edit: D'oh! Must remember not to post half-awake! Sorry, just realized that suggesting changes after the edit period is over is a really dick thing to do! Assuming you make it through to the general list, I'd love to see the introduction of some ambiguity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Precisely my point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I rather like the story behind this. I do. It's a little cliche, but this is sci-fi — a lot of the best ideas are a little cliche. That's okay as long as it's enjoyable.

However, I don't think this article does a very good job of communicating the story. As Moose pointed out, its portrayal of the "de-programmed" humans is frankly cliche, and I think there's more to that than just the Foundation's perspective. Really, there's not much there to imply the story until that final addendum, at which point you're more or less just flat-out telling us.

+ There's also some rather sloppy science in here.
- hide

an unknown material whose molecular structure matches nothing on the Foundation's expanded periodic table of elements

If it has a distinctive molecular structure, chances are very good that it's not an element anyway.

the language centers of SCP-2000-1's brain break down, in a manner similar to agnosia and aphasia

Agnosia and aphasia are symptoms, not a disease, and can be caused by many different pathologies. This sentence makes about as much sense as saying "SCP-2000-1's upper respiratory tract becomes irritated, in a manner similar to a runny nose and a cough": it doesn't actually tell you very much.

- hide

Add that to some grammatical and tonal issues, and I just can't upvote; I'm torn on whether or not to downvote. Sorry. :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because you did have the power to begin with, and now you don't anymore. Simple. No paradox unless the power negates itself retroactively, which would be a far, far weirder thing than just negating itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you don't believe it exists, and it therefore stops existing, how could you have the power to make it stop existing if you didn't have the power to begin with?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It would be a paradox if and only if the power was neutralized retroactively. I don't know if this was what was intended, but I get the impression that temporal shenanigans are within the scope of humanities suppressed meddling.

Even if paradox is not the right word, I can see that it could be desirable to the Greater Powers That Be for some small portion of humanity to stay "awakened". I can even accept that there may be psychic mechanics that make it necessary for a certain general low-level open-mindedness towards the paranormal. It just seems to me that these tablets are a scattershot and unreasonably dangerous way of accomplishing that goal.

A handful of agents, either disguised non-humans or de-brainwashed human collaborators, can screen and pick out candidates for awakening training; making sure that only those receptive to the larger goal of helping the race become more mature get any level of power. I infer that that is what the O5 council is doing in this piece. So far so good, it's all in-universe plausible so far.

On the other hand, these slabs let just anyone come to their senses, making it significantly more likely that the newly reawakened will be prone to pointing out the man behind the curtain. I could possibly accept that they were some pre-containment artifacts that were missed in the planetary set-staging. But once the oversight cell became aware of them, the only move consistent with their goals would be to not let anybody, at any level, even suspect that they existed.

To put it another way: In the context of the alien nanny-state, the circa 2000 anomalies on the mainlist are like parents letting their children have a pocketknife. Yes, they may hurt themselves; but it's also the only way they'll learn to use tools safely. But these tablets are more like letting the kids play with the tablesaw and arc welder. Disaster is nearly certain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If all of humanity believes it has no greater power, and that power makes their beliefs manifest, then they would lose their power, creating a paradox.

How's that a paradox? We have a power, and use that power to remove our ability to use that power. I see no logical issues here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because there is a gestalt power at work. If all of humanity believes it has no greater power, and that power makes their beliefs manifest, then they would lose their power, creating a paradox. This file was inserted into the system by the "aware" circle to keep knowledge of that power "real." Think of it like a carrot on a stick. As long as someone is following it, then humanity can move forward. It's why the indoctrinated members of humanity are allowed to play around with scips in the first place. Acknowledgement of a thing makes the thing real to the human race, who otherwise would have "thought" that thing into nonexistence simply by disbelieving it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, even though (maybe because?) it struck me as a re-telling of Scientology doctrine from Lord Zenu's point of view.

But I dislike the anchor device. I just can't buy that the Foundation Galactica is clever and powerful enough to pull off the Truman Show on a planetary level, but just happens to leave a couple of laptops that default to "Hooked on Phonics - Galspeak" laying around. Viral languages/True Speech is also very well-trodden ground.

There is also an outright hole in the plot. If there is a faction within the Foundation complicit with the containment of humanity, why would they allow this document to be on file? At the very least, they would order that translation is "too hazardous" and have that partial translation sealed away/expunged so it couldn't clue us inmates in on the game.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, I don't think this will win the 2000 contest, but with some very minor additions and rewriting, it could be a very good SCP-001.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't say I care for this. I got largely what you were going for, though the psychic stuff and the preserving the universe bits aren't really in the article. The first part is fairly weak (memetic language that turns people into misanthropic geniuses), and even though that's not really what's going on, the second part doesn't fix it enough. I like the idea of the addendum, but all it does is present the notion in a very straight-forward way. Sorry, but downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a perfectly fair stance to take. =)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd say it was both. The weapon was missing parts and it was probably built with sub-par materials. Like making gunpowder, the quality of the chemicals matters to avoid instability.

I fear I've painted myself into a corner on this story. Revealing too much would spoil the hook, I fear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This could use some tightening for word choice, and the ending is along the same lines that one where the monsters are actually the next stage of human evolution, but the final note is really great. I will say that the object itself isn't all that interesting, and seeing the image followed by "SCP-2000 are personal data assistants" made me wonder just how they reached that conclusion. That said, is the reason the ion cannon in the second log overheated because they stopped the guy making it before he was done?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first, I was not too impressed. Sure, the idea of some super advanced alien race who left some stuff behind that makes the test subject super clever is fun, but I've already seen it plenty of times. that was my first impression. And then I read that last little part at the end of the article, the undergoing translation document. And I had that "Holy shit, that is awesome, did not see it coming" 4 or 5 seconds moments. Well done sir, very well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Everyone's entitled to their opinions. If you have the full story and still decide to downvote that's your choice. I'm happy with this scip and how modestly well it's done, and it fits my headcanon. I'm on Cloud Nine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a piece of entertainment that bores me due to indulging in a cliche without bringing enough new and interesting to the table.

The fact that it bores me may work on some conceptual level, but I'm not voting based on that, or how well this works in-universe. I'm voting on how much it entertains me as a piece of writing. And that ultimately determines how well it works.

because you're creating the perfect grimdark twist to this concept, that the Foundation itself is holding humanity back, and violently so.

Except this is just another cliche, and it's also been done already many, many, many, many times. It's cliches all the way down. Even the 'oh but do you see you have fallen for the perfect twist' is a cliche. Meta-cliche!

Not that you can't use cliches, but ideally you'd still want to make them work in interesting ways. Lowell's contest entry even does the OH BUT DO YOU SEEEEEEE cliche, but IMO makes it work better… although it's lower-rated than this article, so who knows. This article works for enough people that it's well in the positives, so take that for what it's worth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Funny how you only see "pointlessly hostile evil geniuses." That's what the Foundation sees too, and that's why this report reflects that.

Say you were an interstellar ship engineer with a smokin' hot wife and three wonderful kids. Then you black out and wake up as a low-level researcher married to internet porn. You realize the galactic community just brainwashed your species, and that the Foundation isn't just containing anomalies, they're holding humanity prisoner on their own home planet, and you might never see your family again. Would you sit in your cell and cry away the life you lost, or would you-a member of an advanced civilization who knows how to make energized ion gas the way a "modern" kid can look up gunpowder on the internet-try desperately to escape, to wake people up, to pull away the veil lowered over our eyes?

But I'm glad you're expressing the Foundation's perspective, Moose, because you're creating the perfect grimdark twist to this concept, that the Foundation itself is holding humanity back, and violently so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's actually a nice and compelling concept if I understand it correctly:

Spoilers
Hide

all humans are being contained on Earth, and the language returns us to what we are supposed to be.

But I think it's a boring execution. The effect just makes humans into pointlessly hostile super-geniuses? [Phrasing this in non-spoilery fashion.] Bleh. I wish it was something more.

Why is it always extremely pointlessly hostile evil geniuses? Hell, this hasn't been the only time we've seen this on this site.

Downvoted, unfortunately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Using the cancel vote button & then re-voting again seems to solve this (looks like it's already been done).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is weird. Neutral? *Shrug*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is really odd

i have apparently both upvoted and downvoted this article

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: FWIW, I figured all of that out except the part that the Foundation is in-the-know. I think the article does a good enough job of conveying the idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is absolutely my headcannon, I +1 in the hope that you can make that idea less subtle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still think it could come across more clearly in the article itself. Being subtle and allowing readers to get it themselves is great, but not when it's done to such an extent that the reveal happens in the comments rather than the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now this is more like it. I had half figured out the true story before reading your explanation in the discussion. Upvoted with the force of a thousand exploding suns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. If I can't win an upvote I'm happy to live and let live. To be fair, the technical skill isn't meant to be the focus, but I can understand how people would be distracted by it given some of the similar entries on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can I just say how *refreshing* it is that you've thought that far ahead? I +1'd already based on the strength of the article itself. But seeing context this well-developed makes me wish I could make that a +2. Kudos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I initially downvoted based on some of the off-tone stuff in the description and the wacky devices portion of the narrative. I will go along to a certain extent with the "humanity as an SCP" concept, and that humans have some higher state that makes them extremely dangerous. When that manifests as "hey guize, I made a railgun out of dryer lint, a pack of Mentos and an Archie comic", you've completely lost me.

After some reflection, though, I came to the conclusion that this written well enough that its survival on the wiki wasn't an affront to me, and the end note, while not particularly grabbing me, did lend this a little much-needed depth. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the clues are there in the entry. Reading the language, which is actually humanity's "native" tongue, it triggers a breakdown of the Indoctrination process, which has convinced you that you're someone else. The implication is that modern humanity: you, me, and six billion others, are different people. We might have been born on other planets and brought here recently, like within the past decade. The Indoctrination stripped away our lives and implanted memories and languages. You could have been a starship builder with a smokin' hot wife, now you're a car mechanic married to internet porn.

So after exposure to L-2000 all the repressed memories start to come back. It's not a memetic effect like the researchers believe, it's a reset button. Your original personality overrides the programmed one, your language centers reset to their factory defaults. Now you find yourself trapped on a planet forcibly converted like "Invasion of the Body Snatchers." You have superior technical knowledge and psychic abilities that are starting to return. Time to liberate humanity!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if Level 5 Indoctrination is Level 05 Foundation Security. They are privy to the overarching secrets of the Foundation that even the people working for them are not.
